\input{tables/values_from_summary}

\shorttitle{Scale Identifiability in the RAR}
\shortauthors{Licht}

\begin{document}

\title{A Kernel+Scale Identifiability Framework with Anti-Numerology
Controls: Localizing $g^{\dagger}$ in the Radial Acceleration Relation}

\author{Russell Licht}
\affiliation{Independent Researcher}
\email{r.licht@digconsulting.info}

% ============================================================
\begin{abstract}
We introduce and validate a kernel+scale identifiability framework
with built-in anti-numerology controls for testing whether a
characteristic acceleration scale appears in galaxy dynamics data.
Applied to \ValNData\ rotation-curve measurements from the SPARC
database, the baseline fit localizes a preferred scale at
$\log_{10}(s/{\rm m\,s^{-2}}) = \ValBestLogScale$, while
galaxy-grouped \ValCvFoldsTotal-fold cross-validation gives
$\log_{10}(s/{\rm m\,s^{-2}}) = \ValCvMean \pm \ValCvStd$,
consistent with $\gdag \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-10}$~m~s$^{-2}$ to
within \ValDeltaFromGdag~dex.
In the full-data baseline fit, the Bose--Einstein RAR kernel is preferred over six alternatives
(including hyperbolic cotangent, logistic, and power-law controls).
Global permutation and within-galaxy circular shift nulls yield
$p_{\pm 0.10}=\ValAOnePTen$ and $p_{\pm 0.05}=\ValAOnePFive$
with one-sided 95\% Clopper--Pearson bounds
$(\ValAOneCPUpperTen,\ValAOneCPUpperFive)$ from zero hits at both
windows (\ValAOneFracTen\ and \ValAOneFracFive).  A block-permute
bins null gives intermediate rates
(\ValATwoBFracTen, $p_{\pm 0.10}=\ValATwoBPTen$;
\ValATwoBFracFive, $p_{\pm 0.05}=\ValATwoBPFive$).  The within-bin
shuffle is a structure-preserving control (not a null test) and
returns \ValATwoCtrlFracTen\ and \ValATwoCtrlFracFive\ at the two
windows, confirming that the scale preference resides in the
cross-bin monotonic correlation.
Negative controls confirm that the signal collapses under predictor
noise ($\sigma \geq 0.3$~dex), variable warping ($x \to x^{\alpha}$),
and galaxy-label randomization, demonstrating that the optimum is not
a numerical artifact.
Extension to Tian et al.\ (2020) cluster-scale data does not recover
$\gdag$, consistent with the known $\sim\!14\times$ scale offset at
cluster masses; this serves as an explicit null example.
We release the full framework, run folders, and reproducibility
metadata to enable independent extension to non-RAR datasets.
\end{abstract}

\keywords{dark matter --- galaxies: kinematics and dynamics ---
methods: statistical}

% ============================================================
\section{Introduction}\label{sec:intro}

The radial acceleration relation \citep[RAR;][]{McGaugh2016,Lelli2017}
is a tight empirical correlation between the observed centripetal
acceleration $\gobs$ and the baryonic acceleration $\gbar$ in
rotationally supported galaxies.  The relation is well described by
a one-parameter interpolation function with a characteristic
acceleration scale $\gdag \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-10}$~m~s$^{-2}$.

A natural question is whether $\gdag$ is merely a fitting parameter
or a physically meaningful scale that reappears in independent
contexts.  The answer has implications for dark matter models:
modified Newtonian dynamics \citep{Milgrom1983} predicts $\gdag$ as
fundamental, while $\Lambda$CDM must explain its emergence from
galaxy formation physics \citep{Desmond2017,Navarro2017}.

In the Bose--Einstein condensate (BEC) dark matter framework, the
RAR mapping takes the form of a Bose--Einstein occupation number
(R.~Licht, in preparation):
\begin{equation}\label{eq:berar}
  \frac{\gobs}{\gbar} = \frac{1}{1 - \exp\!\bigl(-\sqrt{\gbar/\gdag}\,\bigr)},
\end{equation}
making $\gdag$ the condensation scale of the dark-matter field.

To test whether this scale preference is robust and not a numerical
coincidence, we develop a \emph{kernel+scale identifiability framework}
with three categories of anti-numerology controls:
(i)~scale injection scans measuring how sharply the AIC minimum
localizes the preferred scale;
(ii)~permutation-based null tests verifying that random pairings
do not reproduce the localization; and
(iii)~nearby-scale comparisons against cosmologically motivated
alternatives ($a_\Lambda$, $cH_0$, $cH_0/6$).

The framework is designed to be reusable: given any $(x, y)$ dataset
and a library of candidate kernels, it returns a ranked list of
kernels with scale estimates, significance metrics, and
interpretation guardrails.  We demonstrate it on SPARC as a
validated control and on cluster-scale data as an explicit null.

Throughout, we adhere to a conservative standard: claims are limited
to what the data directly support, negative results are reported in
full, and all outputs are traceable to specific files and random seeds.

% ============================================================
\section{Methods}\label{sec:methods}

\subsection{Kernel Library}\label{sec:kernels}

We define seven candidate kernel functions $K(x, s)$ mapping a
physical variable $x$ (acceleration) and a free scale parameter $s$
to a predicted response $y$:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{BE\_RAR} (Eq.~\ref{eq:berar}): $K = 1/(1 - e^{-\epsilon})$,
        $\epsilon = \sqrt{x/s}$.
  \item \textbf{BE\_occupation}: $K = 1/(e^{\epsilon} - 1)$.
  \item \textbf{BE\_cousin}: $K = \epsilon/(1 - e^{-\epsilon})$.
  \item \textbf{coth}: $K = \coth(\epsilon)$.
  \item \textbf{tanh}: $K = \tanh(\epsilon)$.
  \item \textbf{logistic}: $K = 1/(1 + e^{-(\epsilon-1)})$.
  \item \textbf{power\_law}: $K = \sqrt{x/s} + 1$ (no special scale).
\end{enumerate}

For the SPARC RAR, we use ``direct'' mode: $y = K(x, s)$ with zero
nuisance parameters, because the RAR mapping \emph{is} the kernel.
This gives the fairest comparison: all models have exactly one free
parameter (the scale $s$).

\subsection{Scale Optimization}\label{sec:scaleopt}

For each kernel, we perform a two-stage optimization:
(i)~grid search over $N_{\rm grid}$ log-uniformly spaced trial
scales in $[10^{-13}, 10^{-8}]$~m~s$^{-2}$, followed by
(ii)~bounded scalar minimization (Brent's method) seeded from the
best grid point.

We compute the Akaike Information Criterion
${\rm AIC} = 2k - 2\ln\hat{L}$, where $k$ is the number of free
parameters and $\hat{L}$ is the Gaussian likelihood evaluated at
the RMS residual.  The Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) is
computed in parallel.

\subsection{Scale Injection Scan}\label{sec:scalescan}

To characterize how sharply the data prefer a particular scale, we
evaluate the AIC across 300 trial scales spanning the full search
range.  From this curve we extract:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\daic_{\pm 0.1}$: the AIC increase when the scale is shifted
    $\pm 0.1$~dex from the optimum;
  \item $\daic_{\pm 0.1}/N$: the same, normalized by sample size
    (dataset-independent identifiability metric);
  \item \textbf{Peak sharpness}: the second derivative of the AIC
    with respect to the log-scale at the optimum,
    $\mathcal{S} \equiv d^2({\rm AIC})/d(\log_{10} s)^2 |_{s=\hat{s}}$,
    estimated by finite differences on the scan grid.  Larger values
    indicate a narrower, more tightly localized minimum.  Units are
    AIC per $(\log_{10}\,{\rm m\,s^{-2}})^2$.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Null Tests and Structure-Preserving Control}\label{sec:nulls}

We employ three destructive permutation-based null tests and one
structure-preserving control:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Global shuffle} (A1): permute $y$ relative to $x$,
    destroying all correlation.
  \item \textbf{Block-permute bins null} (A2b): randomly reassign
    which bin's $y$-values go with which bin's $x$-values (0.5-dex
    bins).  This preserves the within-bin marginal distribution of
    $y$ but breaks the cross-bin monotonic relationship that encodes
    the characteristic scale.
  \item \textbf{Within-galaxy circular shift} (A3): for each galaxy,
    circularly shift its $y$ values by a random offset, preserving
    per-galaxy distributions but destroying the radial alignment.
  \item \textbf{Within-bin shuffle} (A2, control): permute $y$ only
    within coarse $x$-bins (0.5~dex width).  This preserves the
    conditional structure $p(y \mid x)$ approximately at the chosen
    bin width and is \emph{not} a null test: $p \approx 1$ is expected
    up to finite-binning and refit variability.
    It confirms that the scale preference resides in the global
    $x$--$y$ correlation, not in bin-local structure.
\end{enumerate}

For each null realization, we refit the best kernel and record the
optimal scale.  The test statistic is
$p_{\pm w} = N_{\rm hits,\pm w}/N_{\rm shuffles}$, where a ``hit''
is $|\log_{10} s_{\rm null} - \log_{10} \gdag| < w$ for
$w \in \{0.10,0.05\}$~dex.  When $N_{\rm hits,\pm w}=0$, we report
the one-sided 95\% Clopper--Pearson upper bound
$p_{{\rm UB},\pm w} = \mathrm{BetaInv}(1-\alpha;\,k+1,\,n-k)$
for $k=N_{\rm hits,\pm w}$ and $n=N_{\rm shuffles}$; for $k=0$ this
reduces exactly to $p_{{\rm UB},\pm w}=1-\alpha^{1/n}$ with
$\alpha = 0.05$.

\subsection{Galaxy-Grouped Cross-Validation}\label{sec:groupcv}

To prevent data leakage (points from the same galaxy appearing in
both train and test), we use GroupKFold with $k=5$ folds where
group~$=$~galaxy.  For each fold we record the best kernel, optimal
scale, and out-of-sample RMSE.

\subsection{Nearby-Scale Comparison}\label{sec:nearby}

We compare $\gdag$ against cosmologically motivated scales:
$a_\Lambda = c^2\sqrt{\Lambda/3}$, $cH_0$, $cH_0/6$ (Verlinde),
$cH_0/(2\pi)$.  This is done in two modes:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{$\eta = 1$ (fixed)}: diagnostic direct substitution
    where the scale is fixed (no optimization) and the likelihood is
    evaluated directly with zero free parameters.
  \item \textbf{$\eta$ free}: fit $y = \eta \cdot K(x, s_{\rm named})$
    with one free parameter per model (matched degrees of freedom).
\end{enumerate}

AIC values from the $\eta=1$ and $\eta$-free modes are not directly
comparable because the parameter count differs; model-selection claims
are restricted to matched-DoF comparisons.

% ============================================================
\section{Data}\label{sec:data}

\subsection{SPARC}

We use the Spitzer Photometry and Accurate Rotation Curves database
\citep{Lelli2016} with standard quality cuts: quality flag
$Q \leq 2$, inclination $30^\circ < i < 85^\circ$, and $\geq 5$
rotation-curve points per galaxy.  This yields $N = 2{,}740$ data
points from 126 galaxies.  The physical variables are
$x = \gbar$~(m~s$^{-2}$) and $y = \gobs/\gbar$ (the RAR mapping
ratio).

\subsection{Tian et al.\ (2020) Cluster RAR}

As a non-RAR pilot, we use the 84-point cluster-scale RAR from
\citet{Tian2020}, comprising 20 CLASH clusters.  The known cluster
acceleration scale is $\sim\!14 \times \gdag$
\citep{Tian2020}; this dataset serves as an explicit
null example where $\gdag$ is \emph{not} expected to be recovered.

% ============================================================
\section{Results}\label{sec:results}

\subsection{Baseline SPARC RAR Control}\label{sec:baseline}

\input{tables/tab_baseline}

The BE\_RAR kernel yields the lowest AIC among all seven candidates,
with an optimal scale of $\log_{10}(s/{\rm m\,s^{-2}})=\ValBestLogScale$,
offset by \ValDeltaFromGdag~dex from $\log_{10}\gdag = -9.921$.

The scale injection scan (Figure~\ref{fig:threepanel}a) shows an
extremely sharp AIC minimum: $\daic/N = \ValDeltaAICPerN$ per data point at
$\pm 0.1$~dex, with peak sharpness
$\mathcal{S} = \ValPeakSharpness$~AIC\,/$(\log_{10}\,{\rm m\,s^{-2}})^2$.

\subsection{Null Tests}\label{sec:nullresults}

\input{tables/tab_nulls}

For global shuffle (A1), hits are \ValAOneFracTen\
($p_{\pm 0.10}=\ValAOnePTen$) and \ValAOneFracFive\
($p_{\pm 0.05}=\ValAOnePFive$), with one-sided 95\%
Clopper--Pearson upper bounds
$p_{{\rm UB},\pm0.10}=\ValAOneCPUpperTen$ and
$p_{{\rm UB},\pm0.05}=\ValAOneCPUpperFive$.  The within-galaxy
circular-shift null (A3) gives the same outcome at both windows
(\ValAThreeFracTen, \ValAThreeFracFive).  The block-permute bins
null (A2b) preserves within-bin heteroskedasticity while disrupting
the cross-bin monotonic mapping, and therefore shows intermediate
behavior: \ValATwoBFracTen\
($p_{\pm 0.10}=\ValATwoBPTen$) and \ValATwoBFracFive\
($p_{\pm 0.05}=\ValATwoBPFive$), as listed in
Table~\ref{tab:nulls}.

The within-bin shuffle (A2) is a \emph{structure-preserving control},
never a null test: it approximately preserves $p(y \mid x)$ within
0.5-dex bins at the chosen bin width.  Accordingly it retains high proximity rates
(\ValATwoCtrlFracTen\ and \ValATwoCtrlFracFive), confirming that the
signal is carried by the global cross-bin mapping rather than by
bin-local marginals alone.

Figure~\ref{fig:threepanel}b shows best-scale distributions for all
four permutation procedures (three nulls + one control).  The global
and galaxy-shift nulls cluster away from $\gdag$.  The block-permute
null is broader with partial overlap near $\gdag$, while the
structure-preserving control retains concentration near $\gdag$.

\subsection{Galaxy-Grouped Cross-Validation}\label{sec:cvresults}

\input{tables/tab_groupcv}

Across \ValCvFoldsTotal\ galaxy-grouped folds, the mean optimal scale is
$\log_{10} s = \ValCvMean \pm \ValCvStd$, consistent with $\gdag$ to
within $1\sigma$.  The scale range across folds is $< 0.1$~dex.
The full-data optimum selects \ValBestKernel; fold-level kernel
selection is \ValCvBestKernelCount/\ValCvFoldsTotal for
\ValBestKernel\ and \ValCvAltKernelCount/\ValCvFoldsTotal for
\ValCvAltKernel.

\subsection{Cut and Grid Sensitivity}\label{sec:sensitivity}

The optimal scale varies by at most \ValCutMaxDelta~dex across four sample
variants (baseline, strict inclination, strict quality, strict
minimum points).  Grid invariance is exact: varying
$N_{\rm grid}$ from 100 to 1{,}000 produces \ValGridMaxDelta~dex
variation (Figure~\ref{fig:stability}).

\subsection{Negative Controls}\label{sec:negcontrols}

\input{tables/tab_negative}

Table~\ref{tab:negative} reports the degradation of the AIC optimum
under five destructive perturbations.  The key results are:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Predictor noise} ($\sigma = 0.3$~dex):
    sharpness decreases substantially and the best scale drifts away
    from $\gdag$.
  \item \textbf{Variable warping} ($x \to x^{0.7}$ or $x^{1.3}$):
    sharpness collapses and the scale displacement is largest.
    This is the most powerful break test in the suite.
  \item \textbf{Galaxy label swap}: sharpness drops and scale drift
    increases relative to baseline.
\end{itemize}

These results confirm that the sharp AIC minimum at $\gdag$ is a
genuine feature of the $\gbar$--$\gobs$ correlation, not a numerical
or methodological artifact.

\subsection{Nearby-Scale Comparison}\label{sec:nearbyresults}

\input{tables/tab_nearby}

At $\eta = 1$ (direct substitution), $\gdag$ is strongly preferred
over $a_\Lambda$.  However, this comparison
tests $\eta = 1$ only and does not rule out $\Lambda$ as the
underlying source of the scale if a non-unity prefactor is allowed.
Because parameter counts differ between $\eta=1$ and $\eta$-free modes,
cross-mode AIC magnitudes are treated as diagnostic rather than as
direct model-selection evidence.

With $\eta$ free (matched degrees of freedom), $cH_0/6$ performs
comparably to $\gdag$, consistent with the Verlinde relation
$\gdag \approx cH_0/6$ (agreement to $\sim\!1.5\%$ in linear
units, 0.039~dex).

\subsection{Non-RAR Pilot: Cluster Data}\label{sec:cluster}

Applied to the Tian et al.\ (2020) cluster RAR, the framework does
\emph{not} recover $\gdag$: the best scale hits the search boundary
at $\log_{10} s = \ValClusterBestLogScale$, offset by
\ValClusterDelta~dex from $\gdag$.  The
$\daic/N$ at $\pm 0.1$~dex is effectively zero
(Figure~\ref{fig:threepanel}c), confirming the absence of
identifiability at galaxy-$\gdag$ in this regime.

This is the expected result: the cluster-scale RAR operates at
$a_0 \approx 14 \times \gdag$ \citep{Tian2020}, and our framework
correctly reports a null.

% ============================================================
\section{Discussion}\label{sec:discussion}

\subsection{What We Claim}

The SPARC RAR strongly localizes a preferred acceleration scale at
$\log_{10} s = \ValCvMean \pm \ValCvStd$ (GroupKFold).  The full-data
optimum selects \ValBestKernel; fold-level kernel selection is
\ValCvBestKernelCount/\ValCvFoldsTotal for \ValBestKernel\ and
\ValCvAltKernelCount/\ValCvFoldsTotal for \ValCvAltKernel.  This
localization survives:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Three destructive null tests plus one structure-preserving
    control (\ValAOneN\ realizations each);
  \item Galaxy-grouped cross-validation (no data leakage);
  \item Quality and inclination cut variations;
  \item Grid resolution changes spanning an order of magnitude.
\end{itemize}
It collapses under destructive perturbations, confirming it is not
a fitting artifact.

\subsection{What We Do Not Claim}

We do not claim detection of $\gdag$ in any context beyond the RAR
itself.  The cluster-scale pilot is an explicit null.  Non-RAR
second-appearance tests (e.g., Tully--Fisher scatter evolution,
weak lensing profile shape, galaxy group catalogs) are in progress;
this work establishes the validated framework and the RAR
identifiability result.

We do not claim that $a_\Lambda$ is ruled out as the origin of
$\gdag$.  Our $\eta = 1$ comparison proves only that the pure
$a_\Lambda$ normalization is disfavored; with a free prefactor,
$cH_0/6$ (which involves $\Lambda$ through the expansion history)
performs comparably.

\subsection{Relation to Prior Work}

The identification of $\gdag \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-10}$~m~s$^{-2}$
in the RAR is not new \citep{McGaugh2016,Lelli2017}.  Our
contribution is a validated, reusable framework that:
(a)~quantifies scale identifiability via $\daic/N$;
(b)~provides multiple null tests including galaxy-grouped permutations;
(c)~includes built-in interpretation guardrails for nearby-scale
comparisons.

\subsection{How to Extend This Framework}

To test whether $\gdag$ appears in a new dataset:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Define $(x, y)$ where $x$ has acceleration dimensions.
  \item Choose a fit mode (``direct'' if $y$ is a known function of
    $K(x,s)$; ``log'' or ``linear'' otherwise).
  \item Run the experiment with the same kernel library and null tests.
  \item A ``second appearance'' detection requires:
    $\daic/N > 0.01$ at $\pm 0.1$~dex,
    $p_{\rm within} < 0.01$ (global shuffle),
    and best scale within $\pm 0.1$~dex of $\gdag$.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Concrete Next Steps}

The following extensions are natural priorities, listed in
approximate order of feasibility:
\begin{itemize}
  \item Apply the framework to independent rotation-curve
    compilations (e.g., BIG-SPARC, when released) with identical
    GroupKFold/null controls.
  \item Map weak lensing excess surface density profiles into
    acceleration space and test for a $\gdag$-scale feature
    (requires sub-35-kpc angular resolution).
  \item Test Tully--Fisher scatter evolution across redshift as a
    state-variable collapse diagnostic.
  \item Run the full kernel+null battery on matched $\Lambda$CDM
    hydrodynamic simulation suites to establish a quantitative
    comparison metric.
\end{itemize}

% ============================================================
\section{Summary}\label{sec:summary}

We have introduced a kernel+scale identifiability framework with
anti-numerology controls.  Applied to the SPARC RAR:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The \ValBestKernel\ kernel localizes the preferred scale to within
    \ValDeltaFromGdag~dex of $\gdag$, with
    $\daic/N = \ValDeltaAICPerN$ at $\pm 0.1$~dex.
  \item Two fully destructive null tests (global, galaxy-shift) yield
    zero hits in both windows:
    A1 (\ValAOneFracTen, \ValAOneFracFive) and
    A3 (\ValAThreeFracTen, \ValAThreeFracFive), with
    CP upper bounds \ValAOneCPUpperTen\ and \ValAOneCPUpperFive.
    The block-permute bins null is intermediate
    (\ValATwoBFracTen, \ValATwoBFracFive), while the
    within-bin structure-preserving control row
    remains high (\ValATwoCtrlFracTen, \ValATwoCtrlFracFive).
  \item Galaxy-grouped \ValCvFoldsTotal-fold CV gives
    $\log_{10} s = \ValCvMean \pm \ValCvStd$, with fold-level kernel
    selection \ValCvBestKernelCount/\ValCvFoldsTotal for \ValBestKernel\
    and \ValCvAltKernelCount/\ValCvFoldsTotal for \ValCvAltKernel.
  \item Negative controls confirm signal collapse under perturbation.
  \item The cluster-scale pilot correctly returns a null.
\end{enumerate}

The framework, code, run folders, and reproducibility metadata are
publicly available upon request and will be hosted in a public
repository upon acceptance.

(ii)~the dataset(s) you intend to use, and

Discussions will prioritize extensions that can be pursued using
publicly available data and the methods described herein.

\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fig1_three_panel.png}
\caption{
\textbf{(a)} AIC vs.\ $\log_{10}({\rm scale})$ for the BE\_RAR
kernel on SPARC, showing a sharp minimum near $\gdag$ (red line).
Reference scales $cH_0/6$ (orange dashed) and $a_\Lambda$ (purple
dotted) are indicated.
\textbf{(b)} Distribution of best-fit scales from \ValAOneN-realization
permutation procedures: global shuffle (gray), block-permute bins null
(blue), within-galaxy circular shift (orange), and within-bin
structure-preserving control (green).  The $\pm 0.1$~dex band
around $\gdag$ is shaded.  For the destructive nulls, A1 and A3
yield \ValAOneFracTen\ and \ValAThreeFracTen\ at $\pm 0.10$ dex,
and \ValAOneFracFive\ and \ValAThreeFracFive\ at $\pm 0.05$ dex.
The block-permute null is intermediate
(\ValATwoBFracTen; \ValATwoBFracFive).  The within-bin
structure-preserving control remains high
(\ValATwoCtrlFracTen; \ValATwoCtrlFracFive).
\textbf{(c)} $\daic/N$ at $\pm 0.1$~dex for SPARC (strong
identifiability) and cluster data (null, as expected).
}
\label{fig:threepanel}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure*}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fig2_validation_stability.png}
\caption{
\textbf{(a)} Galaxy-grouped \ValCvFoldsTotal-fold cross-validation: best scale per
fold.  The $\pm 0.1$~dex band around $\gdag$ is shaded.
Mean $\pm$ std: $\ValCvMean \pm \ValCvStd$.
\textbf{(b)} Sensitivity to quality cuts: baseline ($Q \leq 2$,
$30^\circ$--$85^\circ$, $\geq 5$ pts), strict inclination
($40^\circ$--$80^\circ$), strict quality ($Q \leq 1$), and strict
minimum points ($\geq 10$).  Maximum variation: \ValCutMaxDelta~dex.
\textbf{(c)} Grid invariance: best scale vs.\ $N_{\rm grid}$
from 100 to 1{,}000.  Variation = \ValGridMaxDelta~dex.
}
\label{fig:stability}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure*}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fig3_negative_controls.png}
\caption{
Negative controls (break tests).
\textbf{(a)} Peak sharpness as a fraction of baseline under five
perturbations: predictor noise ($\sigma = 0.1, 0.3$~dex), variable
warping ($x \to x^{0.7}, x^{1.3}$), and galaxy-label swap.
Warping completely destroys the optimum (sharpness $\to 0$).
\textbf{(b)} Scale drift: distance of perturbed best scale from
$\gdag$.  The 0.1~dex threshold (red dashed) is exceeded by warps
and galaxy swap.
}
\label{fig:negcontrols}
\end{figure*}

\section{Reproducibility}\label{app:repro}

\input{tables/appendix_repro}

\noindent The complete run folder, including all stored arrays,
null distributions, and figure-generating scripts, is available in
the project repository.  To regenerate all results:

\begin{verbatim}
python3 analysis/tests/\
  test_gdagger_hunt_refereeproof.py \
  --n-shuffles 500 --seed 42
python3 analysis/tools/\
  make_paper_assets.py
\end{verbatim}
